Gamma Spectroscopy: Angular Distribution, Mixing Ratios, and Polarization

1. Gamma-Ray Angular Distribution

Angular distribution measurements exploit spatial anisotropy of gamma radiation emitted from oriented nuclear states to determine nuclear spins (JJJ) and mixing ratios (δ\deltaδ) by comparing measured radiation patterns with theoretical models.

General Formalism

Nuclear states produced in reactions (e.g., fusion-evaporation) are typically aligned with spin vectors perpendicular to the beam axis. Gamma-ray intensity W(θ)W(\theta)W(θ) is measured at specific angles. Data are fitted to a Legendre polynomial expansion to extract experimental coefficients:

W(θ)exp=A0[1+a2expP2(cos⁡θ)+a4expP4(cos⁡θ)]W(\theta)_{exp} = A_0 [1 + a_2^{exp} P_2(\cos \theta) + a_4^{exp} P_4(\cos \theta)]W(θ)exp​=A0​[1+a2exp​P2​(cosθ)+a4exp​P4​(cosθ)]

Notation:

  • A0A_0A0​: Isotropic normalization constant
  • Pk(cos⁡θ)P_k(\cos \theta)Pk​(cosθ): Legendre polynomials of order kkk
  • akexpa_k^{exp}akexp​: Measured angular distribution coefficients (e.g., a2expa_2^{exp}a2exp​, a4expa_4^{exp}a4exp​)

Theoretical Framework

Calculation of Theoretical Coefficients

Theoretical angular distribution coefficients account for nuclear orientation and transition properties:

aktheo(δ)=Qk(σ)⋅Bk(Ji,Jf,L,L′,δ)a_k^{theo}(\delta) = Q_k(\sigma) \cdot B_k(J_i, J_f, L, L', \delta)aktheo​(δ)=Qk​(σ)⋅Bk​(Ji​,Jf​,L,L′,δ)

Physical Components:

  • Qk(σ)Q_k(\sigma)Qk​(σ): Orientation parameters (quantify nuclear alignment quality)
    • Range: 0≤Qk≤10 \le Q_k \le 10≤Qk​≤1 (unity for perfect alignment)
    • Depend on substate population distribution width σ\sigmaσ
    • Account for deorientation effects from nuclear recoil, precession, or finite detection solid angle
  • Bk(Ji,Jf,L,L′,δ)B_k(J_i, J_f, L, L', \delta)Bk​(Ji​,Jf​,L,L′,δ): Radiation distribution coefficients
    • Tabulated geometrical factors from angular momentum coupling
    • Depend on initial spin JiJ_iJi​, final spin JfJ_fJf​, multipolarities LLL, L′L'L′, and mixing ratio δ\deltaδ

Input Parameters:

  • Known/constrained: JiJ_iJi​, JfJ_fJf​, LLL, L′L'L′, QkQ_kQk​ (from calibration or model)
  • Extracted parameter: Mixing ratio δ\deltaδ (scanned from −∞-\infty−∞ to +∞+\infty+∞)

Experimental Interpretation

  • Dipole transitions (ΔJ=1\Delta J = 1ΔJ=1): Negative a2expa_2^{exp}a2exp​ values (e.g., ≈−0.7\approx -0.7≈−0.7 for pure stretched dipole with full alignment)
  • Quadrupole transitions (ΔJ=2\Delta J = 2ΔJ=2): Positive a2expa_2^{exp}a2exp​ values (e.g., ≈+0.3\approx +0.3≈+0.3 for pure stretched quadrupole with full alignment)
  • Isotropic emission (J=0J=0J=0 or unaligned): a2exp≈a4exp≈0a_2^{exp} \approx a_4^{exp} \approx 0a2exp​≈a4exp​≈0

2. Multipole Mixing Ratio (δ\deltaδ)

Transitions between nuclear states often proceed via a mixture of two multipolarities: LLL (lower order) and L′=L+1L' = L+1L′=L+1 (higher order), such as mixed M1+E2 transitions.

Definition

The mixing ratio δ\deltaδ is defined as the ratio of reduced matrix elements of competing multipoles:

δ=⟨Jf∣∣L′∣∣Ji⟩⟨Jf∣∣L∣∣Ji⟩\delta = \frac{\langle J_f || L' || J_i \rangle}{\langle J_f || L || J_i \rangle}δ=⟨Jf​∣∣L∣∣Ji​⟩⟨Jf​∣∣L′∣∣Ji​⟩​

Properties:

  • Magnitude: Related to intensity ratio via I(L′)/I(L)=δ2I(L')/I(L) = \delta^2I(L′)/I(L)=δ2
  • Sign convention: Critical for comparison; two major conventions exist:
    • Rose-Brink: Common in older literature
    • Krane-Steffen: Standard in modern evaluations
    • Signs are typically opposite depending on matrix element phase definitions

Chi-Squared (χ2\chi^2χ2) Metric

χ2\chi^2χ2 quantifies the goodness of fit between experimental measurements and theoretical calculations.

Formula:

χ2(δ)=∑k=2,4(akexp−aktheo(δ)Δakexp)2\chi^2(\delta) = \sum_{k=2,4} \left( \frac{a_k^{exp} - a_k^{theo}(\delta)}{\Delta a_k^{exp}} \right)^2χ2(δ)=k=2,4∑​(Δakexp​akexp​−aktheo​(δ)​)2

Interpretation:

  • Low χ2\chi^2χ2: Good agreement (theory matches experiment)
  • High χ2\chi^2χ2: Poor agreement (theory contradicts experiment)

Analysis Workflow

  1. Select spin hypothesis: Assume specific Ji→JfJ_i \to J_fJi​→Jf​ transition
  2. Scan δ\deltaδ: Vary δ\deltaδ continuously (often plotted as x=arctan⁡δx = \arctan\deltax=arctanδ from -90° to +90°)
  3. Calculate χ2\chi^2χ2: For each δ\deltaδ step, calculate aktheoa_k^{theo}aktheo​ and resulting χ2\chi^2χ2
  4. Find minimum: The δ\deltaδ value at χmin2\chi^2_{min}χmin2​ is the measured mixing ratio
  5. Determine uncertainty: Range where χ2≤χmin2+1\chi^2 \le \chi^2_{min} + 1χ2≤χmin2​+1 defines 1σ1\sigma1σ uncertainty

arctan⁡(δ)\arctan(\delta)arctan(δ) Analysis

Purpose of Transformation

The mixing ratio δ\deltaδ ranges from −∞-\infty−∞ to +∞+\infty+∞. The arctan⁡(δ)\arctan(\delta)arctan(δ) transformation compresses this infinite range into a finite interval:

  • δ\deltaδ range: (−∞,+∞)(-\infty, +\infty)(−∞,+∞)
  • arctan⁡(δ)\arctan(\delta)arctan(δ) range: (-90°, +90°) or (−π/2,+π/2)(-\pi/2, +\pi/2)(−π/2,+π/2)

Axis Interpretation

The plot sweeps through all possible mixing ratios, from pure lower multipole (LLL) to pure higher multipole (L+1L+1L+1).

arctan⁡(δ)\arctan(\delta)arctan(δ) δ\deltaδ Value Physical Meaning
0° 0 Pure lower multipole (LLL), e.g., pure M1
±45° ±1 Equal mixing (LLL intensity = L+1L+1L+1 intensity)
±90° ±∞ Pure higher multipole (L+1L+1L+1), e.g., pure E2

Data Interpretation

  • χ2\chi^2χ2 plots: Minimum (lowest χ2\chi^2χ2) indicates most likely arctan⁡(δ)\arctan(\delta)arctan(δ) value. Values below confidence limit (e.g., χmin2+1\chi^2_{min} + 1χmin2​+1) define uncertainty range. Assumed spin sequences whose minimum χ2\chi^2χ2 curve lie above this threshold are statistically rejected as incompatible with the experimental data.
  • Coefficient plots: Experimental values (akexp±Δakexpa_k^{exp} \pm \Delta a_k^{exp}akexp​±Δakexp​) appear as horizontal bands; theoretical curves (aktheoa_k^{theo}aktheo​ vs. arctan⁡(δ)\arctan(\delta)arctan(δ)) are calculated functions. Intersection indicates solution. The dashed line typically represents the 99.9% (or 0.1%) confidence limit for rejecting incorrect spin hypotheses.

3. Linear Polarization (PPP)

Angular distributions determine multipolarities (LLL) but are often insensitive to parity change (π\piπ) of transitions (e.g., distinguishing M1 from E1 or E2 from M2). Linear polarization measurements resolve this ambiguity.

Physical Principle

Gamma rays from aligned nuclei are linearly polarized. The electric field vector direction depends on electromagnetic character (electric vs. magnetic) of the radiation.

Measurement Technique

Polarization is measured using Compton polarimeters (often segmented germanium detectors or clover detectors), which exploit Compton scattering probability dependence on incident photon polarization vector.

Asymmetry (AAA)

Experimental asymmetry is defined as:

A=N⊥−N∥N⊥+N∥=Q⋅P(θ)A = \frac{N_\perp - N_\parallel}{N_\perp + N_\parallel} = Q \cdot P(\theta)A=N⊥​+N∥​N⊥​−N∥​​=Q⋅P(θ)

Notation:

  • N⊥N_\perpN⊥​, N∥N_\parallelN∥​: Coincidence counts where scattering plane is perpendicular or parallel to reaction plane
  • QQQ: Polarization sensitivity of detector
  • P(θ)P(\theta)P(θ): Degree of polarization at angle θ\thetaθ (typically measured at 90°)

Parity Determination

For measurements at θ=90°\theta = 90°θ=90°:

  • Electric transitions (E1, E2): Polarization vector perpendicular to beam-detector plane (P>0P > 0P>0)
  • Magnetic transitions (M1, M2): Polarization vector parallel to beam-detector plane (P<0P < 0P<0)

Conclusion: Combining a2expa_2^{exp}a2exp​, a4expa_4^{exp}a4exp​ (spin/multipolarity) with PPP (parity) enables unique JπJ^\piJπ assignments.
